content.problemStatement:
Some programming contest problems are really tricky: not only do they
require a different output format from what you might have expected, but
also the sample output does not show the difference. For an example,
let us look at permutations.

A **permutation** of the integers 1 to n is an
ordering of
these integers. So the natural way to represent a permutation is
to list the integers in this order. With n = 5, a
permutation might look like 2, 3, 4, 5, 1. 

However, there is another possibility of representing a permutation:
You create a list of numbers where the i-th number is the
position of the integer i in the permutation. 
Let us call this second
possibility an **inverse permutation**. The inverse permutation
for the sequence above is 5, 1, 2, 3, 4.


An **ambiguous permutation** is a permutation which cannot be
distinguished from its inverse permutation. The permutation 1, 4, 3, 2
for example is ambiguous, because its inverse permutation is the same.
To get rid of such annoying sample test cases, you have to write a
program which detects if a given permutation is ambiguous or not.



Input Format
The input contains several test cases.

The first line of each test case contains an integer n
(1 ≤ n ≤ 100000).
Then a permutation of the integers 1 to n follows
in the next line. There is exactly one space character
between consecutive integers.
You can assume that every integer between 1 and n
appears exactly once in the permutation.


The last test case is followed by a zero.



Output Format
For each test case output whether the permutation is ambiguous or not.
Adhere to the format shown in the sample output.
constraints: The sum of n over all test cases fits easily into memory limits
Each integer from 1 to n appears exactly once in the permutation
content.inputFormat: The input consists of multiple test cases. Each test case starts with an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 100000). If n is zero the input ends. Otherwise the next line contains a permutation of the numbers 1 … n: n distinct integers separated by a single space. The permutation is guaranteed to contain each integer from 1 to n exactly once.
content.outputFormat: For every test case (except the terminating zero) output a single line containing either "ambiguous" if the permutation equals its inverse permutation, or "not ambiguous" otherwise. The order of the lines must follow the order of the test cases.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: permutation 1 4 3 2 equals its inverse, so ambiguous. Second test case: inverse is 5 1 2 3 4, different, so not ambiguous. Third test case: a single element permutation is always ambiguous.
Permutation 2 3 1 forms a 3‑cycle, its inverse is 3 1 2, thus not ambiguous. Permutation 2 1 swaps the two elements, which is its own inverse, so ambiguous.
Positions 4,5,6 form a 3‑cycle (5→6→4→5), breaking the condition p[p[i]] = i, therefore the permutation is not ambiguous.

solution.approach: For a permutation p (1‑based) its inverse q satisfies q[p[i]] = i. The permutation is ambiguous exactly when p equals its inverse, i.e., for every i we have p[p[i]] = i. We can check this condition in a single linear scan: for i from 1 to n verify p[p[i]] == i. If any position fails the permutation is not ambiguous. The algorithm runs in O(n) time and O(n) extra memory for storing the permutation.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, where n is the size of the current permutation.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) to store the permutation array.
